aliases: လက်ဖက်ထမင်းသုပ်

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Rice dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for Burmese lahpet htamin thoke in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Burmese rice salad preparation; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Tealeaf Rice Salad is approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as sold at Singapore hawker centres and casual dining establishments.

nutrient_source.title: Tealeaf Rice Salad — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tealeaf Rice Salad (cr_id G00010948). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat (6.5 g/100g) is documented in the input health_suitability section referencing HPB data; this value is carried forward. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed rice salad / rice-based salad with condiments (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese Tealeaf Rice Salad. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: mixed rice salad with dressing from the University of Sydney GI Database. The high fat content (44.5 g/100g) from oils and peanuts substantially slows gastric emptying and is expected to suppress the effective GI relative to plain cooked rice (GI ~72). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (56) × available carbohydrate per serving (14.75 g for 250 g portion) / 100 = 8.3. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; potassium only 64 mg/100g, providing negligible offsetting effect
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Tealeaf Rice Salad; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 2025 mg sodium (88% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and the very low potassium-to-sodium ratio further impairs blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (44.5 g/100g) from fried components and oils increases caloric density and promotes insulin resistance; dietary fibre is 0 g/100g providing no glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this dish due to its very high fat load and zero dietary fibre; if consumed, restrict to a small portion (100 g or less) and pair with non-starchy vegetables to partially offset the insulin resistance risk from the high saturated and total fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.5 g/100g combined with sodium 810 mg/100g and cholesterol 26 mg/100g creates a compounded cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of Tealeaf Rice Salad; a single 250 g serving provides 16.25 g saturated fat (exceeding the 20 g daily limit for many adults) and 2025 mg sodium, both of which independently elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure, compounding atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold; saturated fat 6.5 g/100g triggers pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); fried components generate advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); chilli is a nightshade
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the combination of very high sodium, high saturated fat, AGEs from fried garlic and shallots, and nightshade chilli collectively promote joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tealeaf Rice Salad (လက်ဖက်ထမင်းသုပ်, Lahpet Htamin Thoke) is a Burmese-origin rice salad dish served in Singapore hawker centres and casual dining venues. It is made by tossing cooked white rice with fermented tea leaves (lahpet), fried garlic, fried shallots, roasted peanuts, dried shrimp, fish sauce, sesame oil, chilli, and lime juice. The dish has a complex flavour profile: earthy and slightly bitter from the fermented tea leaves, savoury and umami-rich from fish sauce and dried shrimp, crunchy from peanuts and fried aromatics, and mildly spicy from chilli. Nutritionally, it is calorie-dense at 430 kcal/100g, driven by an exceptionally high fat content of 44.5 g/100g from the oils, peanuts, and fried components. Carbohydrate content is relatively low at 5.9 g/100g. Sodium is very high at 810 mg/100g, primarily from fish sauce and dried shrimp. Dietary fibre is 0 g/100g. Saturated fat is 6.5 g/100g. The dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium, high saturated fat, and zero fibre profile. Fermented tea leaves (lahpet) are culturally significant in Myanmar cuisine and provide polyphenols with some antioxidant properties, though these do not offset the dish's adverse nutritional characteristics at typical serving sizes. The dish is not halal due to the presence of dried shrimp and fish sauce, and is not vegetarian or vegan.
common_user_queries: Is tealeaf rice salad suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of tealeaf rice salad
What are the ingredients in Burmese tea leaf rice salad?
Can I eat tealeaf rice salad if I have high blood pressure?
What does lahpet htamin thoke taste like?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010948, confidence 0.95 in source record). Saturated fat value of 6.5 g/100g is referenced in the input health_suitability section consistent with HPB data. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI sub-field only) as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Dietary fibre is reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB source record, consistent with the dish's composition of refined white rice and oil-heavy dressing with minimal fibrous vegetables. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Burmese tea leaf rice salad preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. Saturated fat 6.5 g/100g is carried forward from the input health_suitability section which references HPB data; this value should be confirmed against the full HPB nutrient panel if available. 4. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on mixed rice salad with dressing from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI measurement for lahpet htamin thoke is recommended. 5. The input record incorrectly classifies this dish under primary_cuisine 'Western' and ethnic_tags 'Western'; this has been corrected to Burmese (served in Singapore context) based on the dish's established cultural origin. 6. Halal status is marked false due to the presence of dried shrimp and fish sauce which may not meet halal certification standards depending on preparation source.
